title: Hanging paste-the-wall wallpaper on a feature wall
subtitle: Apply modern paste-the-wall paper to a single wall without a pasting table
excerpt: How to hang paste-the-wall wallpaper on a feature wall. Covers sizing the wall, finding the plumb starting line, applying paste to the wall, and matching the pattern between drops.
sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. Reader's Digest Complete DIY Manual.

glossaryTerms.definition: Wallpaper with a non-woven or synthetic backing that does not need to be soaked. Paste goes on the wall, not the paper, and the drops hang without any soak time.
A vertical reference line drawn or snapped on the wall before papering. Essential because corners and door frames are rarely truly vertical.

The vertical distance after which a pattern motif repeats on the roll. A straight match repeats across a horizontal level; a half-drop match offsets the repeat on alternate drops by half the repeat distance.

content.text: Paste-the-wall paper (also called non-woven or easy-hang paper) is the format most wallpaper comes in now. The paste goes on the wall rather than the paper, which removes the pasting table and the soak-time step from the process. A single feature wall in a standard room takes two to three hours for a first-timer.
Step 1, Prepare the wall

The wall must be sound, dry, and free of grease. Fill any holes and cracks with lightweight filler; sand flush when dry. If the wall has been recently plastered, size it first with diluted PVA (1 part PVA to 5 parts water) applied with a brush and left to dry, fresh plaster is highly absorbent and will dry the paste too fast. Previously painted walls need only to be clean and firm.
Step 2, Plan the drop positions
Identify the
pattern repeat

from the wallpaper label. Count the number of drops the wall needs (wall width divided by roll width, rounded up). Find the centre of the wall and work outward so the pattern is centred, which matters for a bold geometric or a large floral. Draw a vertical
plumb line
at the first drop position using the spirit level. Never work off a door frame or corner, they are almost never plumb.
Step 3, Cut the drops

Measure the drop height (ceiling to skirting board) and add 50 to 75 mm for trim at top and bottom. Cut the first drop. For a patterned paper, align the pattern at the ceiling level. Cut the second drop to match the first across the pattern, cutting from the roll at the correct pattern-repeat offset. Label the back of each drop with a number so you hang them in the right order.
Step 4, Paste the wall and hang the first drop
Mix the paste according to the manufacturer's instructions (most
paste-the-wall

papers need a heavy-duty or fungicidal paste). Apply paste to one drop-width of wall, including 25 mm beyond each edge, with a wide brush or a roller. Do not over-saturate. Lift the first drop and position the top edge against the plumb line, leaving 50 mm overlapping at the ceiling. Smooth from the centre outward and downward with your hand or a wallpaper smoother, pushing out any air bubbles.
Step 5, Trim and continue

Trim the top and bottom with the utility knife, using a broad knife or filling knife as a guide to get a clean line. For subsequent drops, butt the edge of the new drop flush against the previous one, do not overlap. With a patterned paper, slide the drop up or down until the pattern matches across the joint before pressing it to the wall. Paste the wall one drop at a time; do not paste ahead. Wipe paste off the ceiling and skirting board immediately with a damp cloth.
Variations
Small pattern or plain paper: hang from a corner rather than from the centre. Cutting the first drop to run into the corner allows the pattern to flow naturally across the open wall.

Around a window: cut the drop to the approximate shape, hang it, and trim to the reveal. Leave enough overlap at the inner reveal to fold around and fix with a small amount of paste on the reveal face.
Troubleshooter

items.symptom: Paper slipping and not staying aligned
items.cause: Paste too thin or wall over-saturated, reducing grip
items.fix: Mix a stiffer paste and apply it to a drier section of wall.

items.symptom: Bubbles under the paper after it dries
items.cause: Air not smoothed out during hanging
items.fix: Small bubbles usually dry flat within 24 hours. Large bubbles need a small slit with the utility knife, paste injected behind, and the surface pressed flat.

items.cause: Drops cut without checking the repeat offset
items.fix: For future drops, lay each cut drop next to the previous one on the floor before hanging to confirm the match.
